summary:
Aurangzeb's reign (1658–1707 AD) brought the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] to its greatest territorial extent while simultaneously planting the seeds of its [[event:2446|decline]] — a paradox of imperial success and institutional failure that historians have debated for centuries. Aurangzeb, the sixth Mughal emperor, was the most religiously orthodox of the Mughals: he banned music and dance at court, reimposed the jizya tax on non-Muslims (abolished by [[event:2381|Akbar]]), promoted Islamic law, and ordered the destruction of hundreds of Hindu temples (including the Kashi Vishwanath temple at Varanasi) — reversing the syncretic tolerance that had characterized his great-grandfather Akbar's rule and alienating the Hindu majority of the empire's population. His wars against the [[event:2487|Deccan Sultanates]] extended Mughal territory to its maximum, but the 27-year Deccan campaign (1681–1707) consumed resources and manpower at a catastrophic rate.

Aurangzeb's religious policies generated persistent revolts that the empire never fully suppressed. The [[event:2486|Mughal-Sikh conflict]] intensified under his orders (he executed Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji in 1675). The [[event:2368|Maratha Empire]] under Shivaji and his successors proved militarily irrepressible despite Aurangzeb's personal command in the Deccan. The Rajput princes, traditional pillars of Mughal military power, became increasingly alienated and unreliable. By the time of Aurangzeb's death in 1707 at age 88, having spent his last years in the field, the empire was exhausted, financially ruined, and surrounded by enemies — and the subsequent succession wars among his sons accelerated the fragmentation that would reduce the Mughals within a generation to figureheads ruling from the ruins of Delhi.
